Items.Title: 如何在 Ruby on Rails 中实现授权模式？ | How to implement Authorizer pattern in Ruby on Rails?
Items.Description:
在本文中，我们将探索 Ruby on Rails 应用程序中的Authorizer模式。 我们将提供Authorizer模式的定义并讨论其用例和示例。 我们还将深入探讨这种模式的必要性以及它如何帮助解决 Rails 应用程序中的某些问题。

简单来说， Authorizer模式允许我们在 Rails 应用程序中定义和封装复杂的业务规则。 如果不满足此规则指定的要求，则会引发错误。 您可以将Authorizer视为自定义ActiveRecord Validator ，但它不是验证输入数据，而是用于执行业务需求。

为什么我们需要它，这种模式可以解决什么问题？ 让我们看看下面的例子。

 def create user = User.first raise 'User is not active' if user.status != 'active' || user.approved_at.blank? article = Aricle.create(user: user, title: 'title', body: 'body') render json: article end

在下面的示例中，我们希望在调用业务逻辑之前确保完全满足某些要求。

我们对这种方法有什么问题吗？ 是的我们有。

  • 无法与控制器分开进行测试。
  • 它不可重复使用。
  • 我们违反了单一职责原则，因为控制器负责业务规则验证。
  • 以下代码增加了“认知负荷”，需要更多时间来理解。
  • 以下代码不可扩展，很难添加更多规则。

我们如何解决这些问题？ 我们可以将此逻辑移至模型级别并使用before_create回调。
  • 我们可以创建Athorizer类并将逻辑移到那里。

    • 让我们一一看看这些选项。

    带回调的模型

    这是最简单的选择，我们只需要按以下方式扩展我们的模型：

     # app/models/article.rb class Aricle < ApplicationRecord before_save :check_user_status! def check_user_status! raise 'User is not active' if user.status != 'active' || user.approved_at.blank? end end user = User.last Aricle.create(user: user, article_params)

    这种方法有什么缺点吗？

    有2个问题：

    • 我们违反了单一职责 SOLID 原则。 这个模型变得FAT了，要负责太多的事情。
    • 在 Rails 模型中使用回调会使代码更难理解和维护，因为它们与模型紧密耦合且缺乏透明度。 因此，它通常被认为是一个坏模式。

    所以最好将这个逻辑封装在一个单独的类中。 我们该怎么做？

    授权模式

    我们将通过普通的 Ruby 对象实现 Athorizer 模式，因为它是最清晰、最直接的解决方案。

    让我们看看下面的例子。

     # app/authorizers/has_active_user_authorizer.rb class HasActiveUserAuthorizer def initialize(user) @user = user end def authorize! raise 'User is not active' unless active? end private attr_reader :user def active? user.status == 'active' && user.approved_at.present? end end HasActiveUserAuthorizer.new(user).authorize!

    我们只是将我们的规则封装在一个单独的类中，仅此而已。 此代码遵循 SOLID 原则，更易于理解和维护。

    命名约定

    有两种常见的方式来命名授权人：

    • 在命名空间内（例如， Authorizers::HasActiveUser ）
    • 带有后缀（例如， HasActiveUserAuthorizer ）

    但它们有一个共同的规则——授权人必须在其名称中清楚地反映其引入的规则。 让我们看一下授权模式通常使用的其他可能名称以获得更广泛的了解：

    • 公司没有员工授权人

    如果公司至少有一名员工，则引发错误

    • 如果文章获得批准，则引发错误

      • 授权人::有至少一个评论

      如果没有评论则引发错误

      • 授权者::未填充

      如果表中已经填充了记录，则会引发错误

      • 如果用户没有有效订阅则引发错误

        • 如果客户的年龄未获批准，则会引发错误

          将授权模式与其他模式进行比较

          类似于 表单对象

          表单对象和授权者都在运行业务逻辑之前执行验证并引发错误。 那么，有什么区别呢？ 主要区别是：

          • 表单对象验证来自用户输入的数据
          • 授权者验证一般业务逻辑规则

          附言。 要了解有关在 Ruby on Rails 中使用表单对象模式的更多信息，您可以查看这篇文章。

          类似于 策略对象

          授权者也类似于策略对象，因为它们都封装了业务规则。 主要区别是：

          • 授权者可能只包含一个业务规则，但策略对象可以包含多个。
          • 授权者更严格，要求遵循业务规则，而策略对象只会询问规则是真还是假。 这种差异反映在! 由授权人和? 通过 Policy 对象在其方法的末尾。

          附言。 要了解有关在 Ruby on Rails 中使用策略对象模式的更多信息，您可以查看这篇文章。

          类似于自定义验证器

          • 授权者在职责方面类似于自定义 ActiveRecord 验证，但自定义验证器验证一个数据字段，而授权者验证整个模型的通用规则。

          笔记：

          不要将授权模式与授权逻辑混淆。 授权者询问服务是否被授权在当前条件下执行操作，而授权逻辑发生在更高级别（通常在控制器级别）。

          奖励章节：向服务对象添加授权者

          在上一章中，我们提到授权器类似于自定义验证器。 让我们看一下自定义验证器的示例：

           # app/models/article.rb class Article < ApplicationRecord # ... validate :name_presence # ... def name_presence errors.add :base, "Name is empty" if name.blank? end end

          或者在一个单独的类中：

           # app/models/article.rb class Article < ApplicationRecord # ... validates_with NamePresenceValidator # ... end # app/validators/name_presence.rb class NamePresenceValidator < ActiveModel::Validator def validate(record) record.errors.add :base, "Name is empty" if record.name.blank? end end

          本质上，我们希望在服务对象中为我们的授权者提供类似的接口，如下所示：

           # app/services/create_article_service.rb class CreateArticleService # ... authorize 'has_active_user_authorizer' authorize 'another_authorizer' authorize 'one_more_authorizer' # ... end

          我们应该怎么做？ 让我们考虑一下我们需要什么：

          • 服务（保留authorize方法）
          • 具有authorize方法定义的模块（以便能够将其包含在每个服务中）
          • 授权者（封装复杂的业务规则）
          • 基本授权者（以避免为每个新授权者重复相同的代码）

          让我们将它们一一添加。

          注意：我们将只提供代码片段而不做解释以保持简洁。

          服务

          # app/services/create_article_service.rb class CreateArticleService include Authorizable authorize 'has_active_user_authorizer' attr_reader :user, :params def initialize(user: , params:) @user = user @params = params end def call authorize! # main logic end end

          授权模块

          # lib/authorizable.rb module Authorizable extend ActiveSupport::Concern included do class_attribute :authorizers, default: [] end class_methods do def authorize(authorizer_name) self.authorizers += [authorizer_name] end end def authorize! authorizers.each do |authorizer| authorizer.to_s.classify.constantize.new(self).authorize end end end

          授权人

          # app/authorizers/has_active_user_authorizer.rb class HasActiveUserAuthorizer < BaseAuthorizer def authorize! raise 'User is not active' unless active? end private def active? service_object.user.status == 'active' && service_object.user.approved_at.present? end end

          基础授权者

          # lib/base_authorizer.rb class BaseAuthorizer def initialize(service_object) @service_object = service_object end def authorize! raise 'NotImplementedError' end private attr_reader :service_object end

          而已！ 现在，您可以在服务对象中使用授权模式来封装复杂的业务规则，并确保在执行主要逻辑之前遵循这些规则。 要了解有关在 Ruby on Rails 中使用服务对象模式的更多信息，您可以查看我的另一篇文章。

          所以最终的解决方案可能是这样的：

           def create user = User.first UserIsActiveAuthorizer.new(user).authorize! article = Aricle.create(user: user, title: 'title', body: 'body') render json: article end

          或者使用服务对象：

           def create user = User.first article = CreateArticleService.new(user: user, params: { title: 'title', body: 'body' }).call render json: article end

          结论

          • 干燥且可重复使用。
          • 授权者模式使我们能够避免拥有“胖”模型并遵守 SOLID 原则。
          • 它减少了“认知开销”，使代码更容易理解。
          • 它通过在运行任何表单验证或业务逻辑计算之前引发错误来确保尽快满足任何必要的要求。


    原文:

    In this article, we will explore the Authorizer pattern in Ruby on Rails applications. We will provide a definition of the Authorizer pattern and discuss its use cases and examples. We will also delve into the need for this pattern and how it helps to solve certain problems in Rails applications.

    In simple terms, the Authorizer pattern allows us to define and encapsulate complex business rule in our Rails applications. If the requirement specified by this rule is not met, an error will be raised. You can think of an Authorizer as a custom ActiveRecord Validator, but instead of validating input data, it is used to enforce business requirements.

    Why do we need it and what problems can this pattern solve? 🤷🏻‍♂️

    Let's take a look at the following example.

    def create user = User.first raise 'User is not active' if user.status != 'active' || user.approved_at.blank? article = Aricle.create(user: user, title: 'title', body: 'body') render json: article
end

    In the following example, we want to ensure that certain requirements are fully met before calling the business logic.

    Do we have any problems with this approach? 🤔

    Yes, we have.

    • It is not possible to test separately from the controller.
    • It is not reusable.
    • We violate the Single Responsibility Principle because the controller becomes responsible for business rules validation.
    • The following code increases 'cognitive load' and requires more time to understand.
    • The following code is not scalable and it is difficult to add more rules.

    How can we solve these problems? 🤔

    • We can move this logic to the model level and use before_create callback.
    • We can create Athorizer class and move the logic there.

    Let's take a look at these options one by one.

    Model with callback

    This is the easiest option, we just need to extend our model in the following way:

    # app/models/article.rb class Aricle < ApplicationRecord before_save :check_user_status! def check_user_status! raise 'User is not active' if user.status != 'active' || user.approved_at.blank? end end user = User.last
Aricle.create(user: user, article_params)

    Are there any disadvantages to this approach?

    There're 2 problems:

    • We violate the Single responsibility SOLID principle. The model becomes too FAT and responsible for too many things.
    • Using callbacks in Rails models can make the code more difficult to understand and maintain due to their tight coupling with the model and lack of transparency. Therefore, it is generally considered to be a bad pattern.

    So it would be nice to encapsulate this logic in a separate class. How can we do that?

    Authorizer Pattern

    We're going to implement Athorizer Pattern via plain Ruby object because it's the clearest and the most straightforward solution.

    Let's take a look at the following example.

    # app/authorizers/has_active_user_authorizer.rb class HasActiveUserAuthorizer def initialize(user) @user = user end def authorize! raise 'User is not active' unless active? end private attr_reader :user def active? user.status == 'active' && user.approved_at.present? end end HasActiveUserAuthorizer.new(user).authorize!

    We just encapsulated our rules inside a separate class, and that's it. This code adheres to the SOLID principles and is much easier to understand and maintain.

    Naming convention

    There are two common ways to name authorizers:

    • Inside a namespace (for example, Authorizers::HasActiveUser)
    • With a postfix (for example, HasActiveUserAuthorizer)

    But they have one rule in common - the authorizer must clearly reflect in its name the rule it introduces. Let's take a look at other possible names that are commonly used for the Authorizer Pattern to get a wider picture:

    • CompanyHasNoEmployeesAuthorizer

    Raises an error if the company has at least one employee

    • ArticleNotApprovedAuthorizer

    Raises an error if the article is approved

    • Authorizers::HasAtLeastOneComment

    Raises an error if there are no comments

    • Authorizers::NotPopulated

    Raises an error if the table is already populated with records

    • UserHasActiveSubscriptionAuthorizer

    Raises an error if the user does not have an active subscription

    • CustomerAgeApprovedAuthorizer

    Raises an error if the customer's age is not approved

    etc ...

    Comparing the Authorizer Pattern to Other Patterns

    Similar to Form Object

    Form objects and Authorizers both perform validation and raise errors before running business logic. So, what are the differences? The key differences are:

    • Form objects validate data from the user input
    • Authorizers validate general business logic rules

    P.S.. To learn more about using Form object pattern in Ruby on Rails, you can check out this article.

    Similar to Policy Object

    Authorizers are also similar to Policy objects because both encapsulate business rules. The key differences are:

    • Authorizers may contain only one business rule, but Policy objects can contain many.
    • Authorizers are more strict and demand that a business rule be followed, whereas a Policy object will only ask if the rule is true or false. This difference is reflected in the use of ! by Authorizers and ? by Policy objects at the end of their methods.

    P.S.. To learn more about using Policy object pattern in Ruby on Rails, you can check out this article.

    Similar to Custom Validators

    • Authorizers are similar to custom ActiveRecord validations in terms of their responsibility, but custom validators validate one data field, while Authorizers validate general rules for the whole model.

    Note:

    Don't confuse the Authorizer pattern with authorization logic. Authorizers ask if the service is authorized to perform the action under the current conditions, while authorization logic happens at a higher level (usually on the controller level).

    Bonus Chapter: Adding Authorizers to Service Objects

    In the previous chapter, we mentioned that Authorizers are similar to custom validators. Let's take a look at the example of custom validators:

    # app/models/article.rb class Article < ApplicationRecord # ... validate :name_presence # ... def name_presence errors.add :base, "Name is empty" if name.blank? end
end

    Or in a separate class:

    # app/models/article.rb class Article < ApplicationRecord # ... validates_with NamePresenceValidator # ...
end # app/validators/name_presence.rb class NamePresenceValidator < ActiveModel::Validator def validate(record) record.errors.add :base, "Name is empty" if record.name.blank? end
end

    Essentially, we want to have a similar interface for our Authorizers within Service objects, something like this:

    # app/services/create_article_service.rb class CreateArticleService # ... authorize 'has_active_user_authorizer' authorize 'another_authorizer' authorize 'one_more_authorizer' # ...
end

    How can we do this? Let's consider what we need:

    • Service (to keep the authorize method)
    • A module with the authorize method definition (to be able to include it in every service)
    • Authorizer (to encapsulate complex business rule)
    • A base Authorizer (to avoid repeating the same code for every new Authorizer)

    Let's add them one by one.

    Note: We will only provide code snippets without explanation to keep it brief.

    Service 

    # app/services/create_article_service.rb class CreateArticleService include Authorizable authorize 'has_active_user_authorizer' attr_reader :user, :params def initialize(user: , params:) @user = user @params = params end def call authorize! # main logic end end

    Authorizable module 

    # lib/authorizable.rb module Authorizable extend ActiveSupport::Concern included do class_attribute :authorizers, default: [] end class_methods do def authorize(authorizer_name) self.authorizers += [authorizer_name] end end def authorize! authorizers.each do |authorizer| authorizer.to_s.classify.constantize.new(self).authorize end end
end

    Authorizer 

    # app/authorizers/has_active_user_authorizer.rb class HasActiveUserAuthorizer < BaseAuthorizer def authorize! raise 'User is not active' unless active? end private def active? service_object.user.status == 'active' && service_object.user.approved_at.present? end end

    BaseAuthorizer 

    # lib/base_authorizer.rb class BaseAuthorizer def initialize(service_object) @service_object = service_object end def authorize! raise 'NotImplementedError' end private attr_reader :service_object end

    That's it! Now you can use the Authorizer pattern in your service objects to encapsulate complex business rules and ensure that they are followed before the main logic is executed. To learn more about using the Service object pattern in Ruby on Rails, you can check out my other article.

    So the final solution may look like this:

    def create user = User.first UserIsActiveAuthorizer.new(user).authorize! article = Aricle.create(user: user, title: 'title', body: 'body') render json: article
end

    Or using a service object:

    def create user = User.first article = CreateArticleService.new(user: user, params: { title: 'title', body: 'body' }).call render json: article
end

    Conclusion

    • The Authorizer Pattern helps us better separate logic, making it easier to test our code.
    • It is DRY and reusable.
    • The Authorizer Pattern allows us to avoid having a "fat" model and adhere to SOLID principles.
    • It reduces "cognitive overhead", making the code easier to understand.
    • It ensures that any necessary requirements are met as soon as possible by raising an error before running any form validations or business logic calculations.
Items.Author: vladhilko

Items.Title: 使用 F# 和 ML.NET 2.0 在 .NET 中进行机器学习 | Machine Learning in .NET with F# and ML.NET 2.0
Items.Description:
封面图片由Matt Eland使用 MidJourney 创建

机器学习很棒， ML.NET是一个很棒的开源库，它让我们可以编写 dotnet 代码来在我们的生产应用程序中执行机器学习任务。 但是，没有那么多文章可以指导您了解如何使用 F# 或 Polyglot Notebooks 进行机器学习，因此本文旨在解决这个问题。

在本文中，我将概述一个简单的流水线，用于训练回归机器学习模型并将其保存到一个文件中以备后用。 我们将了解如何使用 F# 加载模型并使用它为新数据点生成新预测。

最后，我将向您展示如何在 Polyglot Notebook 中完成所有这些操作，尽管您可以略过实验的这一方面，因为几乎所有代码都可以在 Polyglot 之外的普通.fs文件中正常工作笔记本电脑。

注意：本文适用于F# Advent 2022系列文章，面向熟悉 F# 的读者。 如果您不熟悉 F#，它是一种完全属于 dotnet 语言家族的函数式编程语言。 如果你想要一些C#的资料，更多内容可以看我在ML.NET上的其他内容。

什么是多语言笔记本？

该项目为Jupyter Notebooks添加了一个新内核，并允许您在交互式数据科学笔记本中编写 F#、C#、HTML、SQL 和其他语言。

这使您无需完整的 F# 项目即可编写快速数据科学实验。 这还允许您将 F# 代码和 Markdown 代码混合在一起，以及查看单元格之间的中间数据值。

最终结果是混合了丰富的降价文档、可编辑代码和内联结果的轻量级开发体验。 这使您可以轻松地与您的团队共享小型实验，并允许他们轻松地执行该代码、进行调整并自行调查结果。

本文的其余部分将重点介绍使用 F# 进行机器学习的 ML.NET 特定方面。 如果您更深入地了解 Polyglot Notebooks，或者想要详细的安装说明，请查看我关于 C# 的 Polyglot Notebooks 的文章和视频。

什么是 ML.NET？

随着 ML.NET 2.0 的主要版本最近在 2022 年末发布，ML.NET 得到了积极的维护和发展。此外，ML.NET 正朝着令人难以置信的令人兴奋的方向前进，其地平线上有许多深度学习功能。

虽然大多数数据科学社区目前确实使用 Python 来完成机器学习任务，但 ML.NET 的存在有两个主要目的：

  • 首先，一些开发人员更喜欢将 F# 或 C# 代码编写为 Python 代码，而 ML.NET 让您可以在 dotnet 中训练模型
  • 其次，ML.NET 支持开放神经网络交换 (ONNX) ，允许跨平台和跨语言模型共享。 这使您可以在 Python 中训练模型并使用 dotnet 部署它（反之亦然）

引用 ML.NET

在我们可以使用 ML.NET 做任何事情之前，我们需要添加对该包的引用。

在 Polyglot Notebooks 中，您可以使用以下 F# 代码单元执行此操作：

 // Install the Microsoft.ML packages we're going to use for ML.NET #r "nuget:Microsoft.ML"

运行此程序后，Notebook 将下载相关包并让您知道它安装了Microsoft.ML和相关版本号（撰写本文时为 2.0.0）。

如果您在 Visual Studio 中工作，则可以使用 NuGet 包管理器通过搜索“Microsoft.ML”然后安装该依赖项来手动添加引用。

您还可以在 .NET CLI 中运行以下命令： dotnet add package Microsoft.ML

使用 F# 命名空间

将 ML.NET 添加到项目或笔记本后，添加一个笔记本单元格，该单元格使用几个命名空间的open关键字：

 open Microsoft.ML; open Microsoft.ML.Data;

这允许我们在 Polyglot Notebooks 中使用 F# 中的 ML.NET 类型，而无需使用完全限定名称。

注意：如果您愿意，这也可以是另一个单元格的一部分。 我倾向于将我的 NuGet 语句放在它们自己的单元格中，并将我的open语句放在它们自己的单元格中，因为我倾向于在不同的单元格之间快速迭代，并且 NuGet 步骤比其他步骤花费的时间更长。

检索数据源

我们将在这个项目中进行的确切实验是获取伦敦的历史天气数据，并用它来预测未来的降雨量。 确切的数据来源来自Kaggle.com ，可供下载查阅。

数据集包括以下列：

  • 日期- 记录的测量日期 - (int)
  • 阳光- 以小时为单位的阳光测量（小时） - （浮动）
  • 来自Emmanuel F. Werr的数据集和列描述

您需要做的第一步是下载此London_Weather.csv文件并将其放在与 Jupyter Notebook 相同的目录中。

如果您在 Jupyter Notebook 之外使用代码，请记下您保存文件的磁盘位置，并在以后的步骤中将其提供给您的 F# 代码。

在 F# 中为 ML.NET 输入表示数据

在 ML.NET 中，我们使用称为 IDataView 的IDataView对象来存储和操作数据。 对于具有 Python 背景的人来说，这类似于 Pandas DataFrame。

加载IDataView的一个选项涉及声明每行将包含的类型。

下面是一个 F# 单元格，它定义了一个新的WeatherInput记录，该记录可以存储我们关心的任何给定行的内容。

 type WeatherInput = { // Ignore date column [<LoadColumn(1)>] CloudCover: Single // in oktas [<LoadColumn(2)>] Sunshine: Single // in hours [<LoadColumn(3)>] GlobalRads: Single // Global radiation in Watt / square meter [<LoadColumn(4)>] MaxTemp: Single // Celsius [<LoadColumn(5)>] MeanTemp: Single // Celsius [<LoadColumn(6)>] MinTemp: Single // Celsius [<LoadColumn(7)>] Precipitation: Single // This is the precipitation in millimeters. This is what we want to predict [<LoadColumn(8)>] Pressure: Single // In Pascals // Ignore snow depth since that won't be known at time of prediction and is related to Precipitation }

稍后还将使用此WeatherInput类型从我们训练的模型生成预测。

您可能注意到[<LoadColumn(1)>]语法。 这是一个 F#属性，它告诉 ML.NET 属性应映射到的数据源中从零开始的列索引。 这可能是 ML.NET 代码中我最不喜欢的部分，尽管 ML.NET 确实提供了一些替代方法来推断列类型（如果您愿意）。 但是，我在本文中介绍了基本的 ML.NET 语法。

需要特别注意的一件事是，这些列都是Single或Float32值。 最后，请注意，我没有选择表示 Date 或 SnowDepth 列，因为 Date 列是我不希望模型训练过程知道的列。 我选择忽略那天的积雪深度，因为如果我包含该值，我们将需要在生成第二天的降雨预测时提供它。 因为我们无法提前准确知道降雪量，而且它与降水量密切相关，所以我觉得不知道它对模型训练和预测过程最有用。

将数据加载到 IDataView

现在我们已经声明了数据类型，只需少量代码即可将其加载到IDataView中：

 // Create an ML Context. We'll use this for all ML operations let context = MLContext(); // Load the data let data = context.Data.LoadFromTextFile<WeatherInput>("london_weather.csv", hasHeader = true, separatorChar=',') data.Schema

我们做的第一件事是创建一个新的MLContext实例。 几乎 ML.NET 中的所有内容都流经MLContext实例，因此我们需要一个向前发展的实例。

接下来，我们使用此上下文实例从文件中加载数据。 我们还必须通过separatorChar参数指定逗号分隔符，以便准确加载行。

最后，最后一行代码只是data.Schema ，它获取IDataView的Schema属性。 我们这样做是为了通过 Polyglot Notebooks 可视化加载的模式。 如果您想验证加载的数据以确保它已正确加载，您可以使用Preview函数和该预览的RowView属性查看前 3 行：

 let numRows = 3 data.Preview(numRows).RowView

这个提示将为您节省很多问题。 例如，在撰写本文期间，我遇到了一些问题，导致加载的数据行全为 0，而我没有意识到这一点，因为我没有预览我的数据。

将数据拆分为训练和测试拆分

在机器学习中，通常将数据分成训练和测试部分，以检测记忆了训练数据但对新数据表现不佳的过度拟合模型。

如果您想在训练/测试拆分中使用不同的百分比分配，只需将testFraction调整为其他内容即可。 但是，建议值介于 0.1 和 0.3 之间。

注意：您不必以这种方式拆分数据。 您还可以使用 LoadFromTextFile 加载两个单独IDataView实例，如上所示。 或者，您可以稍后将所有训练数据提供给模型训练步骤，但不建议这样做。 缺乏测试数据集使得更难获得关于模型如何针对新数据执行的准确指标。

使用 ML.NET 在 F# 中训练回归模型

现在您已经加载了训练和测试数据，我们需要构建一个特征化管道，选择一个模型训练算法，将该算法与训练数据相匹配，并评估其指标。

创建特征化管道

下一部分可能是学习 ML.NET 中最令人生畏的部分：创建处理管道。

该流水线是一系列离散步骤，这些步骤接收输入数据并以标准化方式形成，可以由机器学习模型训练过程解释。

为了有效地训练模型，我们需要做以下事情：

  • 确保我们试图预测的值在名为“标签”的列中
  • 确保有助于该预测的特征被聚合到一个名为“特征”的列中
  • 标准化不同列之间的值，以便它们使用标准比例
  • 缓存数据转换以实现更准确和高性能的模型拟合

请注意，当不同的列具有不同的尺度时，归一化步骤尤为重要，因此所有列都有平等的机会影响训练模型。 在我们的数据集中，我们有以摄氏度、瓦特、帕斯卡甚至小时为单位的列。

我们可以使用 ML.NET 用 F# 代码表示这个处理管道，代码如下：

 let featureArray = [|"CloudCover";"Sunshine";"GlobalRads";"MaxTemp";"MeanTemp";"MinTemp";"Pressure"|] let processPipeline = EstimatorChain() .Append(context.Transforms.CopyColumns("Label", "Precipitation")) .Append(context.Transforms.NormalizeMeanVariance("CloudCover", "CloudCover")) .Append(context.Transforms.NormalizeMeanVariance("Sunshine", "Sunshine")) .Append(context.Transforms.NormalizeMeanVariance("GlobalRads", "GlobalRads")) .Append(context.Transforms.NormalizeMeanVariance("MaxTemp", "MaxTemp")) .Append(context.Transforms.NormalizeMeanVariance("MeanTemp", "MeanTemp")) .Append(context.Transforms.NormalizeMeanVariance("MinTemp", "MinTemp")) .Append(context.Transforms.NormalizeMeanVariance("Pressure", "Pressure")) .Append(context.Transforms.Concatenate("Features", featureArray)) .AppendCacheCheckpoint context

这很多，但是根据上面的解释，其中大部分应该是相当简单的。

首先，一切都从一个新的EstimatorChain开始。

接下来，我们在管道过程中将WeatherInput中的Precipitation属性重命名为Label列。

之后，我们采用数字列并根据它们的平均值（均值）和方差对它们的范围进行归一化。 我们不需要重命名这些列，因此您会看到相同的名称列出了两次。

之后，我们将所有特征合并到“特征”列中，我们的估算器将在下一步中使用该列。 特征是指有助于预测标签的任何事物，因此您可以将“特征”一词视为机器学习中的“贡献因素”。

最后，我们让 ML.NET 缓存这些结果，以便在训练过程发生之前计算这些值，从而提高训练过程的准确性和性能。

选择模型

接下来我们需要从context.Regression.Trainers组中选择一个回归模型训练算法。

基本 ML.NET 包的可用选项是：

  • LbfgsPoissonRegression
  • OnlineGradientDescent
  • Sdca

在这三者中，梯度下降是最容易解释的，所以我选择从OnlineGradientDescent开始。

创建新的OnlineGradientDescent训练器并将其添加到新管道的代码如下：

 // Select the algorithm we want let trainer = context.Regression.Trainers.OnlineGradientDescent(labelColumnName = "Label", featureColumnName = "Features") // Generate a training pipeline based on the processing pipeline mixed with the trainer let trainingPipeline = processPipeline.Append trainer

在这里，我们指定了我们想要的算法 ( OnlineGradientDescent ) 以及我们处理管道中包含标签和特征的列的名称。

如果我们愿意，我们还可以为模型自定义各种超参数，其中包括OnlineGradientDescent的以下内容：

  • 学习率
  • 学习率是否随时间下降
  • 损失函数
  • 迭代次数

但是，本文的重点是使用 F# 在 ML.NET 中浏览机器学习，而不是为这个示例问题找到最佳模型。

训练模型

现在我们已经加载了数据并准备好模型训练管道，我们可以将训练管道拟合到训练数据集：

 // Train the model let model = trainingPipeline.Fit trainSet

这会同步使用训练管道和训练数据集，以使用我们选择的算法找到最佳模型。 对我来说，使用这个数据集在我的主要开发机器上花费了 0.1 秒。 这比检查是否安装了 NuGet 包所花费的时间要少得多。 这突出了 ML.NET 的性能，尽管不可否认是在一个小样本数据集上。

我们可以使用这个经过训练的模型来生成新的预测，但在我们这样做之前，我们应该收集一些指标来确定结果模型的实际准确度。

在 F 中评估回归模型

为了确定一个模型有多好（或多坏），我们通过在模型上调用Transform让它为我们的测试数据集生成预测。 这会将多个输入行转换为使用该训练模型进行预测的行。

由于我们从数据集中知道那些日子发生的实际降水量，因此我们可以使用它来通过Evaluate函数来衡量这些预测的准确性。

这将为我们提供一组指标，然后我们可以使用我们的数据科学知识对其进行评估，以确定生成的模型有多好。

我的 F# 代码如下：

 let testResults = model.Transform testSet let testMetrics = context.Regression.Evaluate(testResults, "Label", "Score") testMetrics

由于我用单个变量结束了一个 Polyglot Interactive notebook 单元格，我可以在单元格后面的表格中看到内嵌的指标：

这让我们看到以下标准回归指标：

  • 平均绝对误差 (MAE) - 预测偏离源的平均绝对距离（误差）
  • 均方误差 (MSE) - 平方误差。 这就像 MAE，但极值被放大了
  • 均方根误差 (RMSE) - 根 MSE 类似于 MSE，但其单位更易于使用
  • 注意：这里的LossFunction是 MSE 的副本。 我怀疑 LossFunction 选择了一个主要指标来使用，因为所有 4 个指标都是不同的损失函数

    在这些指标中，您希望 MAE、MSE 和 RMSE 尽可能接近零（表示没有任何错误），而您希望 R 平方尽可能接近 1。

    这些指标并不十分糟糕，但也并不令人惊奇。 对我来说，这些指标表示一个初始模型，但我想研究模型训练过程的不同超参数设置、不同的算法和我的管道中的不同处理步骤。 如果我无法让模型正常运行，您甚至可能需要调查您的数据源，看看是否存在重大数据质量问题，或者只是没有足够的信息来建立准确的模型。

    保存和加载模型

    一旦你有了一个你满意的模型，你应该将该模型保存到磁盘，这样你就可以加载它并使用它来生成预测，而无需在未来重新训练它。

    为了简单起见，我们将模型保存到一个 zip 文件中：

     context.Model.Save(model, data.Schema, "Model.zip")

    这会将模型写入Model.zip文件以及我们的 Polyglot Interactive notebook 或您程序的工作目录中（如果您没有为此代码使用 Notebook）。

    一旦你有了一个你想稍后使用的模型，你可以很容易地加载它：

     let loadedModel, loadedSchema = context.Model.Load "Model.zip"

    在这里，我们一起加载模型和该模型的架构定义。 通常您会发现您根本不需要架构定义，但它是 ML.NET API 的一部分。

    使用 ML.NET 和 F 生成预测

    现在我们有了经过训练的模型，我们可以使用它来生成对新数据的预测。

    为了做到这一点，我们首先创建一个PredictionEngine并告诉它它可以期望什么类型的行以及我们希望它给我们什么类型的行。

    输入类型是我们之前在将数据加载到IDataView时定义的WeatherInput类型，但输出类型将是新的：

     [<CLIMutable>] type WeatherPrediction = { [<ColumnName("Score")>] Precipitation: Single }

    这表示具有Precipitation属性的简单类型。 我们使用ColumnName属性将 F# 的默认“分数”列映射到更容易理解的内容。

    最后，我们需要将CLIMutable放在我们的输出类型上，以便 ML.NET 能够在实例创建后改变值并满足一些编译器要求。 意识到我需要CLIMutable属性才能使输出类型正常工作可能是同时使用 F# 和 ML.NET 最困难的方面，因此希望我的学习之旅能帮助您走上自己的道路。

    现在我们有了WeatherPrediction引用类型，我们可以使用它通过以下 F# 代码创建PredictionEngine ：

     let predictionEngine = context.Model.CreatePredictionEngine<WeatherInput, WeatherPrediction>(model)

    接下来，您可以创建一组假条件，并使用它们根据经过训练的模型获得当天的预测：

     let conditions: WeatherInput = { CloudCover = 4f; Sunshine=5.8f; GlobalRads=50f; MaxTemp=5.2f; MinTemp=1.6f; MeanTemp=3.7f; Pressure=101170f; Precipitation=0f; // Needed for the compiler, but its value is ignored } let prediction = predictionEngine.Predict(conditions) prediction

    这会产生一个WeatherPrediction对象，其中包含给定WeatherInput的预测降水量。 将prediction作为单元格中的最后一个值也会导致 Polyglot Notebooks 显示它以方便我们使用：

    换句话说，对于我们的天气情景，我们的模型根据我们对其他天气因素的条件和预测预测将下雨 1.018 厘米。

    结束语

    我们刚刚完成了使用 F# 和 Polyglot Notebooks 在 ML.NET 2.0 中执行基本实验所需的所有基本步骤。

    我发现 Polyglot Notebooks 在为本文生成代码时非常有用。

    此代码与我为完成相同任务而编写的 C# 代码非常相似，因此我认为，如果我在 F# 中经常执行此任务，我可能会开始引入更多功能。 我提到这一点主要是因为写一篇 F# 文章而不在这句话之外使用|>运算符并且不声明我自己的任何函数感觉很奇怪。

    然而，F# 确实让我们能够快速声明类型，忽略 ML.NET 中流行的泛型语法中围绕预测引擎的大量冗长，并完全专注于我们想要完成的任务。

    使用我们拥有的这个最终模型，我们可以将它保存到磁盘，在应用程序启动时加载它，并以可重复和高性能的方式将它集成到任何专业的 dotnet 应用程序中。



原文:

Cover image created by Matt Eland using MidJourney

Machine learning is awesome, and ML.NET is a fantastic open-source library that lets us write dotnet code to perform machine learning tasks in our production applications. However, there aren't as many articles walking you through how to do machine learning using either F# or Polyglot Notebooks, so this article aims to fix that.

In this article I'll outline a simple pipeline that trains a regression machine learning model and saves it to a file for use later on. We'll look at how to load the model using F# and use it to generate new predictions for new data points.

To round things out, I'll be showing you how to do this all in a Polyglot Notebook, though you can skim over this aspect of the experiment as almost all of the code will work just fine in a normal .fs file outside of Polyglot Notebooks.

Note: This article was intended for the F# Advent 2022 series of articles for an audience familiar with F#. If you're not familiar with F#, it's a functional programming language that's fully part of the dotnet family of languages. If you want some C# materials, see my other content on ML.NET for more content.

What are Polyglot Notebooks?

Polyglot Notebooks are the new name of Microsoft's .NET Interactive Notebooks project. This project adds a new kernel to Jupyter Notebooks and allows you to write F#, C#, HTML, SQL, and other languages in an interactive data science notebook.

This allows you to write quick data science experiments without needing a full F# project. This also allows you to mix together F# code and Markdown code as well as see intermediate data values between cells.

The end result is a lightweight development experience that mixes rich markdown documentation, editable code, and inline results. This allows you to easily share small experiments with your team and allows for them to easily execute that code, make tweaks, and investigate the results on their own.

The rest of this article will focus on the ML.NET specific aspects of Machine Learning using F#. If you're curious about Polyglot Notebooks in more depth, or want detailed instructions for installing it, check out my article and video on Polyglot Notebooks for C#.

What is ML.NET?

ML.NET allows you to train and use machine learning models on the dotnet platform. ML.NET is open-source and cross-platform. ML.NET is actively maintained and growing with the major ML.NET 2.0 release occurring just recently in late 2022. What's more, ML.NET is heading in incredibly exciting directions with many deep learning features on its horizon.

While the majority of the data science community does currently use Python for machine learning tasks, ML.NET exists for two major purposes:

  • First, some developers prefer to write F# or C# code to Python code, and ML.NET lets you train models in dotnet
  • Secondly, ML.NET supports Open Neural Network eXchange (ONNX) which allows cross-platform and cross-language model sharing. This lets you train a model in Python and deploy it using dotnet (or vice versa)

Referencing ML.NET

Before we can do anything with ML.NET, we'll need to add a reference to that package.

In Polyglot Notebooks you can do this with the following F# code cell:

// Install the Microsoft.ML packages we're going to use for ML.NET
#r "nuget:Microsoft.ML"

Once you run this, the Notebook will download the relevant package and let you know it installed Microsoft.ML and the relevant version number (2.0.0 at time of writing this article).

If you are working in Visual Studio, you can use NuGet Package Manager to add the reference manually by searching for "Microsoft.ML" and then installing that dependency.

You can also run the following command in the .NET CLI: dotnet add package Microsoft.ML

Working with F# Namespaces

Once you have ML.NET added to your project or notebook, add a notebook cell that uses the open keyword for a few namespaces:

open Microsoft.ML;
open Microsoft.ML.Data;

This allows us to use ML.NET types from F# in Polyglot Notebooks without needing to use fully-qualified names.

Note: This could also be part of another cell if you want it to be. I tend to have my NuGet statements in a cell of their own and my open statements in their own cells since I tend to rapidly iterate between different cells and the NuGet steps take longer than other steps.

Retrieving a Data Source

The exact experiment we'll be doing in this project is to take historical weather data for London and use it to predict rainfall in the future. The exact data source comes from Kaggle.com and is available for download and inspection.

The dataset includes the following columns:

  • date - recorded date of measurement - (int)
  • cloud_cover - cloud cover measurement in oktas - (float)
  • sunshine - sunshine measurement in hours (hrs) - (float)
  • global_radiation - irradiance measurement in Watt per square meter (W/m2) - (float)
  • max_temp - maximum temperature recorded in degrees Celsius (°C) - (float)
  • mean_temp - mean temperature in degrees Celsius (°C) - (float)
  • min_temp - minimum temperature recorded in degrees Celsius (°C) - (float)
  • precipitation - precipitation measurement in millimeters (mm) - (float)
  • pressure - pressure measurement in Pascals (Pa) - (float)
  • snow_depth - snow depth measurement in centimeters (cm) - (float)

Dataset and column descriptions from Emmanuel F. Werr

The first step you need to do is download this London_Weather.csv file and place it inside the same directory as your Jupyter Notebook.

If you are using code outside of a Jupyter Notebook, make note of the location on disk you save the file and provide that to your F# code instead in future steps.

Representing Data in F# for ML.NET Input

In ML.NET we use a DataFrame object called an IDataView to store and manipulate data. This is similar to a Pandas DataFrame for those with a Python background.

One option for loading an IDataView involves declaring the type that each row will contain.

Here is an F# cell that defines a new WeatherInput record that can store the contents we care about for any given row.

type WeatherInput = { // Ignore date column [<LoadColumn(1)>] CloudCover: Single // in oktas [<LoadColumn(2)>] Sunshine: Single // in hours [<LoadColumn(3)>] GlobalRads: Single // Global radiation in Watt / square meter [<LoadColumn(4)>] MaxTemp: Single // Celsius [<LoadColumn(5)>] MeanTemp: Single // Celsius [<LoadColumn(6)>] MinTemp: Single // Celsius [<LoadColumn(7)>] Precipitation: Single // This is the precipitation in millimeters. This is what we want to predict [<LoadColumn(8)>] Pressure: Single // In Pascals // Ignore snow depth since that won't be known at time of prediction and is related to Precipitation }

This WeatherInput type will also be used later on for generating predictions from our trained model.

You likely noted the [<LoadColumn(1)>] syntax. This is a F# Attribute which tells ML.NET the zero-based index of the column in the data source the property should map to. This is maybe my least favorite part of ML.NET code, though ML.NET does offer some alternatives to infer column types if you prefer. However, I'm covering the basic ML.NET syntax in this article.

One particular thing to note is that these columns are all Single or Float32 values. ML.NET is very particular on the data types you give it and it doesn't like double values in particular, so this data choice is deliberate to avoid errors later on in the process.

Finally, note that I did not choose to represent the Date or SnowDepth columns since the Date column was one that I didn't want the model training process to be aware of. I chose to omit the depth of snow on that day because if I included that value, we'd need to provide it when generating rainfall predictions for the next day. Because we can't know the snowfall accurately in advance and it is tightly correlated to precipitation, I felt it would be most useful for the model training and prediction process to not know about it.

Loading Data into an IDataView

Now that we have our data type declared, loading it into an IDataView can be done with a trivial amount of code:

// Create an ML Context. We'll use this for all ML operations
let context = MLContext(); // Load the data
let data = context.Data.LoadFromTextFile<WeatherInput>("london_weather.csv", hasHeader = true, separatorChar=',')
data.Schema

The first thing we do is create a new MLContext instance. Almost everything in ML.NET flows through a MLContext instance, so we need one going forward.

Next we load the data from a file using this context instance. ML.NET lets us use hasHeader to indicate that the first row is just a header and should not be treated as data. We also must specify the comma delimiter via the separatorChar parameter in order to have rows loaded accurately.

Finally, the last line of code is just data.Schema which grabs the Schema property of the IDataView. We do this to visualize the loaded schema via Polyglot Notebooks. Polyglot Notebooks lets you specify values on the last line of any code cell and will automatically log out a display of that object below it as shown below:

If you wanted to proof your loaded data to make sure it was properly loaded, you could take a look at the first 3 rows using the Preview function and the RowView property of that preview:

let numRows = 3
data.Preview(numRows).RowView

This tip will save you a lot of issues. During the writing of this article, for example, I encountered a few issues causing rows to be loaded with all 0's for data and I hadn't realized it because I didn't preview my data.

Splitting the Data into a Train and Test Split

In Machine Learning it is customary to split your data into training and test splits in order to detect overfit models that have memorized the training data but don't perform well against new data.

F# lets us split any IDataView into two parts using the TrainTestSplit method as shown in the code cell below:

// Split into train and test splits to detect overfitting
let split = context.Data.TrainTestSplit(data, testFraction = 0.2) let testSet = split.TestSet // 20 % of the data (0.2)
let trainSet = split.TrainSet // 80 % of the data (0.8)

If you wanted to use a different allocation of percentages with your train / test split, just tweak testFraction to be something else. However, values between 0.1 and 0.3 are recommended.

Note: You don't have to split your data this way. You could also load two separate IDataView instances using LoadFromTextFile as shown above. Alternatively, you could give all of your training data to the model training step later on, but this is not recommended. The lack of a test dataset makes it harder to get accurate metrics of how your model performs against new data.

Training a Regression Model in F# using ML.NET

Now that you have your training and test data loaded, we'll need to build out a featurization pipeline, select a model training algorithm, fit that algorithm to the training data, and evaluate its metrics.

Creating a Featurization Pipeline

The next part is maybe the most intimidating part of learning ML.NET: creating a processing pipeline.

This pipeline is a sequence of discrete steps that take in input data and form it in a standardized way that can be interpreted by a machine learning model training process.

In order to train a model effectively, we'll need to do the following things:

  • Ensure the value we're trying to predict is in a column called "Label"
  • Ensure the features that contribute to that prediction are aggregated into a column called "Features"
  • Normalize values between different columns so they use a standard scale
  • Cache data transformations for more accurate and performant model fitting

Note that the normalization step is particularly important when different columns have different scales so all have an equal chance at impacting the trained model. In our data set we have columns in degrees Celsius, Watts, Pascals, and even hours.

We can represent this processing pipeline in F# code with ML.NET with the following code:

let featureArray = [|"CloudCover";"Sunshine";"GlobalRads";"MaxTemp";"MeanTemp";"MinTemp";"Pressure"|] let processPipeline = EstimatorChain() .Append(context.Transforms.CopyColumns("Label", "Precipitation")) .Append(context.Transforms.NormalizeMeanVariance("CloudCover", "CloudCover")) .Append(context.Transforms.NormalizeMeanVariance("Sunshine", "Sunshine")) .Append(context.Transforms.NormalizeMeanVariance("GlobalRads", "GlobalRads")) .Append(context.Transforms.NormalizeMeanVariance("MaxTemp", "MaxTemp")) .Append(context.Transforms.NormalizeMeanVariance("MeanTemp", "MeanTemp")) .Append(context.Transforms.NormalizeMeanVariance("MinTemp", "MinTemp")) .Append(context.Transforms.NormalizeMeanVariance("Pressure", "Pressure")) .Append(context.Transforms.Concatenate("Features", featureArray)) .AppendCacheCheckpoint context

This is a lot, but most of these should be fairly straightforward given the explanations above.

First of all, everything starts with a new EstimatorChain.

Next, we rename the Precipitation property in WeatherInput to the Label column during the pipeline process.

After that we take the numeric columns and normalize their ranges according to their average (mean) and variance. We do not need to rename these columns so you see the same names listed twice.

After that, we merge all of our features together into the Features column which our estimator will use in the next step. Features refers to anything that contributes to the label being predicted, so you could look at the word "features" as "contributing factors" in machine learning.

Finally we have ML.NET cache these results so that these values are calculated before the training process occurs which improves the accuracy and performance of our training process.

Selecting a Model

Next we need to select a regression model training algorithm from the context.Regression.Trainers group.

The available options with the base ML.NET package are:

  • LbfgsPoissonRegression
  • OnlineGradientDescent
  • Sdca

Of these three, gradient descent is the easiest to explain, so I chose to start with OnlineGradientDescent.

The code for creating a new OnlineGradientDescent trainer and adding it to a new pipeline is as follows:

// Select the algorithm we want
let trainer = context.Regression.Trainers.OnlineGradientDescent(labelColumnName = "Label", featureColumnName = "Features") // Generate a training pipeline based on the processing pipeline mixed with the trainer
let trainingPipeline = processPipeline.Append trainer

Here we specify the algorithm we want (OnlineGradientDescent) as well as the name of the columns in our processing pipeline that contain the label and features.

If we wanted to, we could also customize the various hyperparameters to the model, which include the following for OnlineGradientDescent:

  • Learning Rate
  • Whether learning rate decreases over time
  • Loss Function
  • l2 Regularization
  • Number of Iterations

However, the focus of this article is to tour machine learning in ML.NET with F#, not to find the most optimal model to this sample problem.

Training the Model

Now that we have our data loaded and the model training pipeline ready to go, we can fit the training pipeline to the training dataset:

// Train the model
let model = trainingPipeline.Fit trainSet

This synchronously uses the training pipeline with the training dataset to find the optimal model using the algorithm we selected. For me this took 0.1 second on my primary development machine with this dataset. This is significantly less time than it takes to even check if NuGet Packages are installed. This highlights ML.NET's performance, though admittedly on a small sample dataset.

Once the Fit process is complete, the resulting model is stored in model as an ITransformer.

We can use this trained model to generate new predictions, but before we do that, we should gather some metrics to determine how accurate the resulting model actually is.

Evaluating a Regression Model in F

To determine how good (or how bad) a model is, we have it generate predictions for our test dataset by calling Transform on the model. This transforms multiple input rows to rows with predictions using that trained model.

Since we know from our dataset the actual precipitation that occurred on those days, we can use this to gauge the accuracy of those predictions via the Evaluate function.

This will give us a set of metrics that we can then evaluate using our data science knowledge to determine how good the resulting model is.

My F# code for this is as follows:

let testResults = model.Transform testSet let testMetrics = context.Regression.Evaluate(testResults, "Label", "Score") testMetrics

Since I'm ending a Polyglot Interactive notebook cell with a single variable, I can see the metrics inline in a table following the cell:

This lets us see the following standard regression metrics:

  • Mean Absolute Error (MAE) - the average absolute distance the prediction was off from the source (error)
  • Mean Squared Error (MSE) - the squared error. This is like MAE, but extreme values are amplified
  • Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) - the root MSE is similar to the MSE but its units are easier to work with
  • R Squared - the coefficient of determination is a value indicating how useful this model is for predicting new values

Note: LossFunction here is a duplicate of MSE. I suspect LossFunction selects one primary metric to use since all 4 of these metrics are different loss functions

In these metrics you want your MAE, MSE, and RMSE to be as close to zero as possible (indicating no errors whatsoever) while you prefer your R Squared to be as close to 1 as possible.

These metrics aren't quite terrible, but they're not amazing either. To me, these metrics indicate an initial model to start with, but I'd want to investigate different hyperparameter settings for the model training process, different algorithms, and different processing steps in my pipeline. If I couldn't get a model to perform suitably well, you might even need to investigate your data source and see if there are significant data quality issues or just not enough information for an accurate model.

Saving and Loading the Model

Once you have a model you are happy with, you should save that model to disk so you can load it and use it to generate predictions without needing to retrain it in the future.

ML.NET supports a number of different formats including zip files and ONNX files. To keep things simple, we'll just save our model to a zip file:

context.Model.Save(model, data.Schema, "Model.zip")

This writes the model to a Model.zip file alongside our Polyglot Interactive notebook or in your program's working directory if you're not using a Notebook for this code.

Once you have a model you'd like to use later on, you can load it just as easily:

let loadedModel, loadedSchema = context.Model.Load "Model.zip"

Here we load both the model and a schema definition for that model together. Often you'll find you don't need the schema definition at all, but it's part of the ML.NET API.

Generating Predictions using ML.NET and F

Now that we have a trained model, we can use it to generate predictions for new data.

In order to do this, we first create a PredictionEngine and tell it what type of rows it can expect and what type of rows we want it to give us.

The input type is the WeatherInput type we defined earlier when loading data into an IDataView, but output type will be something new:

[<CLIMutable>]
type WeatherPrediction = { [<ColumnName("Score")>] Precipitation: Single
}

This represents a simple type that has a Precipitation property. We use the ColumnName attribute to map from F#'s default "Score" column to something more easily understandable.

Finally, we need to put CLIMutable on our output type in order for ML.NET to be able to mutate values after instance creation and to satisfy some compiler requirements. Realizing I needed the CLIMutable attribute to get the output type working properly was probably the hardest aspect of working with F# and ML.NET together, so hopefully my learning journey helps you on your own path.

Now that we have our WeatherPrediction reference type, we can use it to create a PredictionEngine with the following F# code:

let predictionEngine = context.Model.CreatePredictionEngine<WeatherInput, WeatherPrediction>(model)

Next, you can create a fake set of conditions and use them to get a prediction for that day based on the trained model:

let conditions: WeatherInput = { CloudCover = 4f; Sunshine=5.8f; GlobalRads=50f; MaxTemp=5.2f; MinTemp=1.6f; MeanTemp=3.7f; Pressure=101170f; Precipitation=0f; // Needed for the compiler, but its value is ignored
} let prediction = predictionEngine.Predict(conditions) prediction

This yields a WeatherPrediction object with the predicted precipitation for that given WeatherInput. Putting prediction as the last value in a cell causes Polyglot Notebooks to display it for our convenience as well:

In other words, for our weather scenario our model predicts it's going to rain 1.018 centimeters given the conditions and predictions we have for the other weather factors.

Closing Thoughts

We just walked through all the basic steps you'd need to perform a basic experiment in ML.NET 2.0 using F# and Polyglot Notebooks.

I found Polyglot Notebooks to be incredibly helpful when generating the code for this article.

This code is very similar to the C# code I'd write to accomplish the same tasks, so I think that if I did this task frequently in F# that I would likely start introducing more functions. I mention this primarily because it feels odd to write an F# article without using the |> operator outside of this sentence and without declaring any functions of my own.

However, F# did get us the ability to quickly declare types, ignore a lot of the verbosity in the generics syntax prevalent in ML.NET around prediction engines, and focus entirely on the task we wanted to accomplish.

With this final model that we have, we can save it to disk, load it upon application start up, and integrate it into any professional dotnet application in a repeatable and performant way.

Items.Title: 如何使用 IP 地理定位数据库获得更好的转化率 | How to get better conversion rate with an IP Geolocation database
Items.Description:
网站开发者可以利用 IP 地理定位数据库来识别网站访问者所在的国家、地区或城市。 但是，IP 地理定位数据库不仅仅可以对特定 IP 地址进行地理定位。 可以利用 IP 地理定位数据库中的数据向其网站添加一些强大的地理定位相关功能。

使用 IP 地理定位数据库的好处

优化网站加载时间
用户的 Internet 连接速度等高级信息对于网站优化目的很有用。 想象一下，一位潜在客户正在浏览您的网站以购买商品。 如果由于用户端的网速较慢而导致您的网站加载时间过长怎么办？ 是的，你已经猜到了。 用户不会费心购买任何东西。 他们只会去您竞争对手的网站进行购买。

值得庆幸的是，IP 地理定位数据库可以识别并存储用户 IP 地址的连接类型和速度。 网站可以避免在其页面中显示占用大量带宽的元素； 即，视频或动画甚至高清图像，适合连接类型较慢的用户。 在这种情况下，使用静态低清晰度图像代替视频是可以接受的。

这种类型的优化可以显着改善在线客户的浏览和用户体验。 毕竟，当他们一开始几乎无法加载时，谁会在乎您的网站是否超级华丽。

本地化网站内容
通过使用 IP 地理定位数据，网站开发人员现在可以个性化 Web 内容以满足区域访问者的需求。

将网站内容本地化有助于提高用户参与度，因为观众将能够轻松找到与他们相关的项目。 这对在线商店来说非常有用，因为它们可以推广通常由同一地区的用户购买的商品。 例如，为来自沿海地区的游客展示沙滩用品或冲浪板。

此外，地理定位允许进一步本地化，例如区域定价、最近的商店方向和当地商店的联系方式。 一般来说，本地化有助于让客户在浏览您的在线商店时感觉更舒适。

当客户在您的在线商店中浏览和搜索产品时拥有良好的体验，他们将更有可能进行购买。

通过在线广告增加网站流量
当在线广告与受众相关时，它们能够产生更多的点击和流量。 这就是 IP 地理定位数据库派上用场的地方。

在有针对性的营销活动期间通过地理定位本地化广告可确保活动的投资回报率很高。 广告可以定位访问您网站的特定客户群，并将他们重定向到特定位置的登录页面。

可以调整此类登陆页面以满足区域客户的品味和要求。 除此之外，他们还可以通过展示店内促销活动轻松增加到最近实体店的客流量。

防止网站上的恶意活动

IP geolocation database stores the latitude and longitude coordinates of a given IP address. Website developers can utilize IP geolocation database to identify the country, region or city of the website visitors. However, an IP geolocation database can do more than just geolocate specific IP addresses. It is possible to leverage the data from an IP geolocation database to add some powerful geolocation-related features to their websites.

Benefits of using IP Geolocation database

Optimizing website loading time
Advanced information like the Internet connection speed of the user is useful for website optimization purpose. Imagine a prospective customer browsing your website for items to purchase. What if your website takes a long time to load due to the slower Internet speed on the user’s side? Yup, you’ve guessed it. The user is not going to bother to buy anything. They’ll just go to your rival’s website to make their purchases.

Thankfully, IP geolocation database identifies and stores the connection types and speeds of the users’ IP addresses. Websites can avoid displaying bandwidth-heavy elements in their pages; i.e., videos or animations or even high-definition images, for users with slower connection types. In such cases, it is acceptable to use static low-definition images instead of videos.

This type of optimization can dramatically improve the browsing and user experience for your online customers. After all, who cares if your website is super flashy when they can barely load it in the first place.

Localized website content
IP geolocation database provides a comprehensive list of detailed info regarding the country, region, city and ZIP code of the potential users. By using IP geolocation data, website developers can now personalize the web content to cater to regional visitors.

Localizing the website content helps to improve user engagement as the audience will be able to easily find items that are relevant to them. This is great for online stores as they can promote items normally bought by users in the same region. E.g., showing beach ware or surfboards for visitors coming from coastal regions.

In addition, geolocation allows further localization such as regional pricing, nearest store directions and local store’s contact details. In general, localization can help to make the customer feel more comfortable browsing on your online stores.

When customers have good experience browsing and searching for products on your online store, they will be more likely to make purchases.

Boost your website traffic from online ads
Online advertisements are able to generate more clicks and traffic when they are relevant to the audience. That’s where the IP geolocation database comes in handy.

Localising ads via geolocation during targeted marketing campaigns ensures that ROI for the campaigns will be high. Ads can target specific groups of customers that visit your site and redirect them to location-specific landing pages.

Such landing pages can be tuned to cater to the taste and requirements of the regional customers. Besides that, they also make it easy to boost walk-in traffic to the nearest physical store by displaying its in-store promotions.

Prevent malicious activities on website
Use IP geolocation data to understand where your website visitors are coming from and how they access your site. Online stores are frequently subjected to online attacks or attempted hacks from malicious parties.

By checking the attacker’s geolocation information, it is possible to limit or mitigate their nefarious actions. Some attacks are designed to hobble websites from operating optimally, potentially hampering the actual web visitors’ browsing and purchasing experience.

Blocking selected users based on their IP geolocation information is an easy way to ensure only legitimate web traffic gets through. That means real customers will not be blocked from performing their online shopping on your store.

Conclusion

The use of IP geolocation data in the e-commerce industry is becoming widespread and common nowadays. There are many use cases for using IP geolocation data to boost sales conversion on online stores with a new range of techniques. Above are only some of the common uses that many web merchants have implemented to provide the best user experience possible for customers.

Don’t underestimate how utilizing an IP geolocation database can help to boost your store sales revenue. Get the free IP2Location LITE databases today to get started.

  • 数据科学和机器学习：随着企业越来越依赖数据做出明智的决策，对具有数据科学和机器学习技能的专业人员的需求预计将继续增长。

  • 全栈开发：精通前端和后端开发的全栈开发人员可能需求量很大，因为企业正在寻找能够构建和维护完整 Web 应用程序的专业人员。

  • 网络安全：随着企业面临的网络威胁越来越多，对具有网络安全技能的专业人员的需求可能会继续增长。

  • 人工智能和机器学习：随着人工智能和机器学习在各个行业中变得越来越普遍，对具备这些领域技能的专业人员的需求可能会很高。

  • 项目管理：有效管理项目的能力可能仍然是自由职业者的一项宝贵技能，因为企业正在寻找能够按时并在预算范围内交付项目的专业人员。

  • 用户体验设计：随着企业专注于创造用户友好的产品和服务，对具有用户体验设计技能的专业人员的需求预计将继续增长。

  • 移动开发：随着越来越多的企业开发移动应用程序，对移动开发人员的需求可能会继续增长。

  • 区块链：随着区块链技术的日益普及，对具有该领域技能的专业人员的需求可能会继续增长。

Items.Title: 如何使用 Jest 进行 JavaScript 代码测试 | How to use Jest for JavaScript code testing
Items.Description:
测试是软件开发过程的重要组成部分。 它有助于确保您编写的代码可靠、可维护且无错误。 在本文中，我们将介绍使用 Jest 进行测试的基础知识。 我们将从了解为什么测试很重要开始，然后继续安装和配置 Jest。 最后，我们将看一些如何使用 Jest 编写和运行测试的示例。

为什么要测试？

测试在软件开发中很重要的原因有很多。 测试的一些主要好处包括：

  • 及早发现错误：通过为您的代码编写测试，您可以在开发过程中及早发现错误，以免它们有机会造成严重问题。

  • 确保代码质量：测试可以帮助确保您的代码是高质量的，方法是检查代码是否按照预期的方式工作以及是否按预期运行。

  • 促进重构：测试可以让重构代码变得更容易，因为它可以让您确信所做的更改不会破坏现有功能。

  • 记录你的代码：测试可以作为你的代码的文档，通过提供应该如何使用它以及它应该做什么的例子。

安装和配置 Jest

要开始使用 Jest，您需要将其作为开发依赖项安装在您的项目中。 您可以通过在终端中运行以下命令来执行此操作：

 npm install --save-dev jest

安装 Jest 后，您需要配置它来运行您的测试。 您可以通过在项目的根目录中创建一个 jest.config.js 文件并添加以下代码来完成此操作：

 module.exports = { testMatch: ['**/*.test.js'], };

此配置告诉 Jest 查找扩展名为 .test.js 的文件并将它们视为测试文件。

编写和运行测试

现在 Jest 已安装和配置，您已准备好开始编写和运行测试。 下面是一个简单测试示例，用于检查 add 函数是否返回正确结果：

 function add(a, b) { return a + b; } test('adds 1 + 2 to equal 3', () => { expect(add(1, 2)).toBe(3); });

要运行此测试，您可以在终端中使用 jest 命令：

您可以在Jest 文档中找到有关这些功能的更多信息。

结论

在本文中，我们介绍了使用 Jest 进行测试的基础知识。 我们已经了解了为什么测试很重要，如何安装和配置 Jest，以及如何编写和运行测试。 通过使用 Jest 和其他测试工具，您可以确保您的代码可靠、可维护、无 bug

Why Test?

There are many reasons why testing is important in software development. Some of the main benefits of testing include:

  • Finding bugs early: By writing tests for your code, you can catch bugs early on in the development process, before they have a chance to cause serious problems.

  • Ensuring code quality: Tests can help ensure that your code is of high quality, by checking that it does what it's supposed to do and that it behaves as expected.

  • Facilitating refactoring: Tests can make it easier to refactor your code, by providing confidence that the changes you make won't break existing functionality.

  • Documenting your code: Tests can serve as documentation for your code, by providing examples of how it should be used and what it should do.

Installing and Configuring Jest

To get started with Jest, you'll need to install it as a dev dependency in your project. You can do this by running the following command in your terminal:

npm install --save-dev jest

Once Jest is installed, you'll need to configure it to run your tests. You can do this by creating a jest.config.js file in the root of your project, and adding the following code:

module.exports = { testMatch: ['**/*.test.js'],
};

This configuration tells Jest to look for files with a .test.js extension and treat them as test files.

Writing and Running Tests

Now that Jest is installed and configured, you're ready to start writing and running tests. Jest tests are written using the describe and test functions.

Here's an example of a simple test that checks that the add function returns the correct result:

function add(a, b) { return a + b;
} test('adds 1 + 2 to equal 3', () => { expect(add(1, 2)).toBe(3);
});

Jest will automatically find and run all of the test files in your project, and report the results.

There are many other features and assertions available in Jest, such as support for asynchronous code, snapshot testing, and mocking. You can find more information about these features in the Jest documentation.

Conclusion

Items.Title: 回归基础 | Returning To The FUNdamentals
Items.Description:
对不起，我利用了乐趣。 不是很抱歉，而是有点抱歉。

无论如何，在过去的七个月左右的时间里，我一直在学习网络开发，并且取得了稳步的进步。 逐渐咬下更大的块，弄清楚如何咀嚼它们，并尝试从编程世界的信息流中喝一杯。

在学习了几个月的基本后端概念（HTTP、路由、wtf 是 Express 以及我如何 fetch() 它？）之后，我又回到了一些基础知识。 为我的狗（嗨，Pickle！ ）制作一个超级基本的 HTML/CSS 登录页面，使用一些 codewars kata 来研究 JavaScript 基础知识，所有这些爵士乐。

我想起了我学到了多少——还有多少我还需要学习。 在这一点上，我觉得我已经掌握了一些更大的概念。 我了解有关 Web 堆栈基本组件的基本知识……前端、后端，我接触过 MySQL 一两次……基础知识，对吗？ 我创建了一个服务器并设置了简单的路由，这些路由实际上将数据发送到我也编写的前端和从前端发送数据。 我用 CSS 设计了一些页面的样式。 不好，请注意。 我很快了解到我不是伟大的设计师。

我已经快速浏览了整个堆栈，现在我回到原点 - 完善基础知识。 我可以让我的元素更具语义吗？ 我的班级名字不那么晦涩难懂？ 我的 JavaScript 明显不那么迟钝？ 我可以！

专注于基础感觉很好，句号。

话虽如此，我也开始学习 Angular……请发送您的祝福和阅读建议，我会需要它们！

原文:

I'm sorry I capitalized the fun. Not a lot sorry, but a little sorry.

Anyhow, I've been learning web development for the past seven months or so and making steady progress. Progressively biting off bigger chunks and figuring out how to chew 'em and trying to get a drink from the information firehose that is the world of programming.

After a several-months long stint of learning about basic backend concepts (HTTP, routing, wtf is Express and how do i fetch() it?), I've wound my way back to some basics. Making a super basic HTML/CSS landing page for my dog (hi, Pickle!), working on JavaScript basics with some codewars kata, all that jazz.

I'm reminded of how much I've learned - and how much I still need to learn. At this point I feel like I have a few of the bigger picture concepts down. I know basic bits about the base components of the web stack... frontend, backend, I touched MySQL once or twice... basics, right? I've created a server and set up simple routes that actually send data to and from the frontend that I also wrote. I've styled some pages with CSS. Not well, mind you. I've quickly learned that I am no great designer.

I've take a quick tour of the whole stack, and now I'm back to square one - refining the fundamentals. Can I make my elements more semantic? My class names less obscure? My JavaScript significantly less obtuse? I can!

Focusing on the fundamentals feels good, full stop.

That being said I also started learning Angular... please send your well wishes and reading recommendations, I'm gonna need them!
